title: Audit-log continuity: detect indexing gap indicating lost evidence
exclusions: Does NOT detect confidentiality compromise of log content itself, nor physical tampering of the indexer host; see UC-22.35.2 (indexer integrity) and UC-22.35.3 (log-content confidentiality).
evidence: Saved search 'audit_log_gap_detection' (daily) outputs a per-source gap report to the compliance summary index; dashboard panel 'Audit log continuity — last 30d' on the evidence-pack dashboard.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Detects interruption of the audit trail (the 'ability to ensure the ongoing ... resilience of processing systems'), but the clause also requires confidentiality and integrity guarantees not covered here.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.32(1)(b) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.35.1: Audit-log continuity: detect indexing gap indicating lost evidence.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.35.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI 10.2.1 requires that audit logs are enabled and active for all system components; this UC detects loss of telemetry but does not validate 10.2.1's sub-requirements (10.2.1.1-10.2.1.7) for specific event types.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When any ingest source known to produce continuous events stops producing events for more than 60 seconds (and no planned-maintenance window is active), the UC fires within the next search interval and names the affected source.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Normal event-rate jitter (up to 30-second gaps), or a source in a pre-declared maintenance window (tag=maintenance), does NOT fire the UC.

dataSources: _internal metrics.log (group=per_sourcetype_thruput), _audit (action=indexing), or any index-time metric that exposes per-source event rates.
app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform (native; no separate TA required) (Splunkbase)
spl:
| tstats count as events where index=_internal source=*metrics.log* group=per_sourcetype_thruput earliest=-30m by sourcetype _time span=1m
| eventstats avg(events) as avg_events, stdev(events) as stdev_events by sourcetype
| where events=0 AND avg_events>0
| eval gap_seconds=60
| table _time sourcetype events avg_events stdev_events gap_seconds
| sort - _time
description: Detects any sourcetype that was previously producing events but has stopped indexing for more than a rolling 60-second window. A regression in event ingestion is the earliest indicator that the audit trail required by almost every modern regulation has been compromised — whether by accident (agent failure, network partition, licensing quota) or by intent (attacker clearing logs).
value: Without continuous audit logs, compliance claims are unfalsifiable. Auditors across GDPR, HIPAA, PCI DSS, SOC 2, and SOX all treat log-gaps as a finding. This UC lets a customer show the auditor a green dashboard: 'here is every second of the last 30 days, accounted for by source.'
implementation: (1) Schedule every 5 minutes; (2) route hits to a restricted summary index (compliance_summary); (3) wire to ITSI service health or ES notable depending on deployment; (4) maintain a maintenance-window lookup (maintenance_windows.csv) listing tagged sources that should NOT alert. Indexes required: `index=_internal`.
visualization: Timeline of event rate per sourcetype with red bars for gap periods; heat-map of sources-by-day with green/amber/red cells; single value for '% of last 30d with full coverage'.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK — T1562.008 Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Logs

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `_internal` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Data Model Acceleration**: The CIM datamodel referenced in the search must be accelerated. Validate with `| rest /services/admin/summarization | search summary.id=*` and confirm `summary.complete=100`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.32(1)(b)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.32(1)(b) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.35.1: Audit-log continuity: detect indexing gap indicating lost evidence." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `_internal` under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Schedule every 5 minutes. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** route hits to a restricted summary index (compliance_summary). Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 3:** wire to ITSI service health or ES notable depending on deployment. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** maintain a maintenance-window lookup (maintenance_windows.csv) listing tagged sources that should NOT alert. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

`eventstats` attaches a group-level aggregate to every row in the result set without collapsing rows. For "Audit-log continuity: detect indexing gap indicating lost evidence" this gives each event per-peer context — a row can be compared to its group's median, average, or quantiles without losing the row-level fields the detection relies on.

For "Audit-log continuity: detect indexing gap indicating lost evidence," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of GDPR control activity.

The grouping `by sourcetype, _time` is the analytical unit — the aggregate is computed within each distinct combination. Note that `_time` appears in the group-by with a `bin span=1m` preceding it — without the `bin`, `_time` would have near-1:1 cardinality with events and the window stat would be trivial. A common mistake is grouping by a field that has cardinality near 1:1 with events, which produces a group-per-event and makes the window stat trivially equal to the event value.

Memory sensitivity: `eventstats` must hold the entire result set in memory to compute per-row aggregates. For searches returning >1M events, this can OOM the search head. If you see `fatal=true` in `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd` or mrsparkle restarts, reduce the base-search time window or pre-aggregate before `eventstats`.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.35.1: Audit-log continuity: detect indexing gap indicating lost ev]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30m
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.35.1,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=_internal sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `sourcetype`, `_time`. Run:

```spl
index=_internal sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table sourcetype, _time
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `avg_events`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(avg_events) as p50 perc90(avg_events) as p90 perc99(avg_events) as p99 max(avg_events) as hi
```

The current threshold `0` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.35.1: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Timeline Of Event Rate Per Sourcetype With Red Bars For Gap Periods; Heat-Map Of Sources-By-Day With Green/Amber/Red Cells; Single Value For '% Of Last 30D With Full Coverage' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We watch for breaks in the audit log stream so you can fix gaps before compliance or security reviews find them first.
